cases.title: Unaccompanied Minor - Syrian Refugee

story.situation: Amira was 14 years old when she arrived at the Port of Entry in Lebanon in March 2024. She had been traveling alone for eight months—a journey that began when her family home in Aleppo was destroyed b...

timeline.event: Family home destroyed in Aleppo; parents arrange departure
Amira arrives at port of entry, detained for immigration processing
Initial social worker interview; vulnerability assessment conducted
Tuteur (Hélène) officially appointed by protective services
Medical evaluation; tuberculosis screening, immunization plan started
Asylum application filed with legal representation
Enrolled in secondary school (10th grade equivalent) with language support
Psychological evaluation for trauma screening; referred to counseling
First school month completed; forming friendships
Asylum interview conducted by government official
Asylum granted; permission to remain and work permit issued
Began part-time internship at local library; continued schooling
First family video call in 14 months (arranged through Red Crescent)

key_events.description: Family home destroyed in Aleppo; parents arrange departure
Amira arrives at port of entry, detained for immigration processing
Initial social worker interview; vulnerability assessment conducted
Tuteur (Hélène) officially appointed by protective services
Medical evaluation; tuberculosis screening, immunization plan started

legal_framework.law_or_regulation: UN Convention on the Rights of the Child (CRC, 1989)
legal_framework.application: Amira is entitled to protection, care, and legal representation as a minor

legal_framework.law_or_regulation: Convention Relating to the Status of Refugees (1951)
legal_framework.application: Amira qualifies for refugee status based on persecution in Syria
Defines refugee status and subsidiary protection in European context
Mandates appointment of tuteur; establishes guardianship responsibilities
Protects minors from exploitation; provides safe harbor provisions
Guarantees free primary and secondary education regardless of immigration status
Entitles all persons to emergency and essential medical care

solutions_implemented.outcomes: Legal protection established; consistent adult advocate
Infection resolved; weight gain of 8kg over 3 months; clearance for school
Strong case documented; asylum granted in January 2025
DALF A2 proficiency reached; able to function in school and community
Regular attendance; grades improved from 45% to 68%; peer friendships forming
Stable living situation; cleanliness standards met; peer relationships supportive
Sleep patterns improved; anxiety episodes reduced by 60%; engagement in activities
First family contact in 14 months; monthly calls established; morale significantly improved
Earned first wages; gained job skills; built professional confidence

tuteur_obligations.description: Coordinated with lawyer, ensured Amira understood asylum proceedings, attended all interviews and hearings
Accompanied Amira to all medical appointments, ensured she understood treatment plans, monitored medication compliance
Maintained contact with school, assisted with homework, advocated for ESL services, attended parent-teacher conferences
Managed small stipend provided by state, taught budgeting basics, ensured food security and necessities
Monitored shelter conditions, reported concerns to social services, arranged transfer when needed, maintained inspection records
Regular visits (minimum twice weekly), listening to concerns, providing encouragement, modeling stable adult relationship
Helped bridge cultural gaps, explained local customs, connected Amira with Arabic-speaking community members, attended cultural events together
Facilitated family reconnection process, helped write letters/emails, coordinated video calls, translated important messages
Kept detailed records of progress, filed required reports with social services, prepared documentation for asylum hearing and school records

lessons_learned: **Early Intervention Saves Lives** - Identifying Amira's vulnerability at border intake triggered a protective cascade; without this, she might have been trafficked or deported
**Trauma-Informed Approach Essential** - Simple interview techniques (speaking in her language, respecting her pace) built trust; aggressive questioning early on would have caused withdrawal
**Tuteur Relationship Transforms Outcomes** - Personal, consistent adult advocacy was as important as legal documents; Hélène became the stable anchor Amira needed
**Medical Care Cannot Wait** - Treating infection and malnutrition immediately improved her mental clarity and ability to engage in other processes
**Education is Stability** - School provided structure, peer relationships, and hope; it was both practical need and psychological lifeline
**Language Access Non-Negotiable** - Arabic-speaking staff and counselors were critical; even professional interpreters cannot replace native speakers for sensitive conversations
**Family Connection Restores Humanity** - The first video call with her parents was transformative; Amira shifted from survival mode to planning for the future
**Documentation Takes Months** - Legal processes are slow; patience and persistence matter; without sustained effort, cases fall through bureaucratic cracks

cases.title: Family Conflict and Child Welfare - Afghan Minor

story.situation: Zahra was 12 years old when her father brought her to a social services office in Brussels and told a caseworker he could no longer care for her. It was October 2024, and her family had arrived as Afg...

timeline.event: Taliban takeover of Kabul; family begins escape planning
Family arrives in Belgium; asylum application filed
Asylum status approved; housing and stipend provided
Zahra enrolled in school; language support classes begin
Father's depression worsens; conflict escalates at home
Crisis moment: father's harsh words to Zahra
Father brings Zahra to social services; protective intervention initiated
Initial social worker assessment; foster placement authorized
Zahra placed with foster family (Anna and Michel)
First supervised visit with father; emotional reunion
Father assigned to trauma-specialized psychologist
Weekly therapy begins for father; medication initiated
Zahra shows behavioral improvement with foster family
Supervised visits increased to twice weekly
Father's stability improves; reintegration planning begins
Zahra moves back with father; weekly social worker support begins
Zahra and father report improved communication and safety
School reports improved academic engagement; peer relationships forming

key_events.description: Taliban takeover of Kabul; family begins escape planning
Family arrives in Belgium; asylum application filed
Asylum status approved; housing and stipend provided
Zahra enrolled in school; language support classes begin
Father's depression worsens; conflict escalates at home

problems_identified.problem: Lack of Professional Mental Health Care

legal_framework.law_or_regulation: UN Convention on the Rights of the Child (CRC, 1989)
legal_framework.application: Zahra has right to family unity; separation only if necessary for protection
Mandates intervention when child welfare at risk; prioritizes family reunification
Establishes rights for refugee families to live together
Refugees entitled to psychological services regardless of status
All children have right to free education despite family status changes
Establishes minimum standards for foster placements; oversight requirements
Family unity principle applies to recognized refugees
Records of family services protected; privacy maintained

solutions_implemented.outcomes: Zahra protected; father given space to stabilize
Father stabilizes; medication prescribed; coping mechanisms develop
Relationship maintained; communication improves; trust rebuilds
Food security achieved; housing improves; reduced stress
Zahra stabilized; peer relationships with foster siblings; school improvement
Math performance excellent; language skills improve; engagement returns
Anger management improves; sleep patterns normalize; hope emerges
Gradual, supported reintegration; risks mitigated
Living conditions improve; separate sleeping areas for daughter; comfort increases
Father connects with peers; Zahra finds cultural community; isolation decreases

tuteur_obligations.description: Conducted comprehensive home visit, interviewed both father and daughter separately, documented risk factors, created immediate safety plan
Coordinated with child protection court; ensured legal authority for placement; maintained ongoing custody documentation
Screened and matched appropriate foster family; provided orientation to Zahra's background and needs; maintained regular communication with Anna and Michel
Identified trauma-specialized psychologist for father; coordinated with Zahra's school counselor; tracked progress on both mental health fronts
Arranged, scheduled, and sometimes supervised father-daughter visits; tracked emotional responses; documented progress
Assessed family's financial needs; helped secure increased refugee stipend; connected to emergency assistance programs; budgeted resources
Evaluated current housing; helped secure better accommodation; ensured meeting basic safety standards; conducted quality inspections
Liaison with school; ensured ESL services; kept both parents informed of academic progress; addressed behavioral concerns
Developed comprehensive, phased plan for family reunification; identified risks; built in safeguards; scheduled gradual transition steps

lessons_learned: **Parental Mental Health Crisis is Child Welfare Crisis** - Untreated trauma in parents directly endangers children; identifying and treating parental mental health is child protection
**Separation as Last Resort Can Be Protective** - Temporary separation allowed both father and daughter healing time; was not punitive but preventive
**Family Reunification Should Be the Goal** - When parents ask for help, they often want to be better parents; support them toward that goal rather than permanent separation
**Supervised Visitation Bridges Separation** - Maintaining father-daughter connection during crisis prevented permanent attachment damage
**Cultural Community Matters** - Connecting father with other Afghan refugees reduced isolation and provided understanding that therapeutic support alone couldn't
**Trauma-Specialized Treatment Works** - Father's willingness to engage with a psychologist who understood refugee trauma was critical turning point
**Multiple Support Systems Needed** - No single intervention (therapy, housing, money, school support) alone would have succeeded; coordinated, comprehensive approach essential
**Hope is Powerful Medicine** - When Zahra saw her father improving and heard him speak about their future, her own healing accelerated

cases.title: Severe Trauma and PTSD - Congolese Minor

story.situation: Karim was nine years old when he was rescued from an armed group in the Democratic Republic of Congo. He had been with them for six months—six months that he could barely articulate, even months later...

timeline.event: Armed conflict; Karim separated from family and abducted by militia
In militia compound; exposed to violence, deprivation, abuse
Rescued by humanitarian organization; taken to medical facility
Medical intake; physical assessment; severe malnutrition and trauma noted
Identity recorded; basic security provided; observation begins
Trauma screening conducted; PTSD symptoms documented
Dr. James (trauma specialist) begins presence-based work with Karim
Art therapy begins; Karim expresses through drawing
Karim begins speaking in short phrases; vocabulary rebuilding
Family identification begun by Red Cross
Contact established with mother; alive and searching for Karim
First video call with mother and sister (ICRC facilitated)
TF-CBT therapy begins; structured trauma processing sessions start
Karim shows improved sleep patterns; nightmares reduced 40%
Repatriation arrangements finalized; transition planning complete
Karim travels home with ICRC social worker support
Reunited with mother and sister in Bukavu
Follow-up therapy and support coordination; community-based care begins

key_events.description: Armed conflict; Karim separated from family and abducted by militia
In militia compound; exposed to violence, deprivation, abuse
Rescued by humanitarian organization; taken to medical facility
Medical intake; physical assessment; severe malnutrition and trauma noted
Identity recorded; basic security provided; observation begins

legal_framework.law_or_regulation: UN Convention on the Rights of the Child (CRC, 1989)
legal_framework.application: Karim has right to protection, recovery, and family reunification

legal_framework.law_or_regulation: Convention on the Worst Forms of Child Labor (ILO)
legal_framework.application: Protects against forced recruitment into armed groups

legal_framework.law_or_regulation: UN Optional Protocol on Children in Armed Conflict
legal_framework.application: Prohibits use of children in warfare; provides demobilization support
Mandates protection of vulnerable children; family reunification priority
Establishes informed consent, trauma-informed care standards
Protects kidnapped children; mandates humane treatment during repatriation
Child has right to education; remedial schooling should be available
Trauma-focused therapy, psychiatric care, medication as appropriate

solutions_implemented.outcomes: Weight gain (4kg in 8 weeks); infections cleared; physical health restored
Trust established; communication begins; processing starts
Safety felt; boundaries respected; foundation of trust built
Emotional release; narrative emerges through images; catharsis
Nightmares reduce 40%; anxiety symptoms decrease; coping skills develop
Better sleep quality; reduced hypervigilance; emotional regulation improves
Family located alive; reunification possible; hope restored
Safe journey arranged; mother prepared; community resources identified
Mother equipped to support; expectations realistic; compassion enhanced
Safety net in place; local therapists trained; follow-up scheduled

lessons_learned: **Relationship is the Primary Intervention** - Healing from trauma first requires safety in relationship; therapeutic techniques are secondary to being present with the child
**Non-Verbal Expression is Essential** - Children traumatized into silence need art, play, and movement; words come later, after safety is established
**Pacing is Critical** - Pushing trauma disclosure too quickly causes re-traumatization; slow, steady, child-led pace allows integration and healing
**Bodily Safety Comes First** - Physical health (nutrition, sleep, medicine) must be addressed before or alongside psychological work
**Patience Rewires Trauma** - Consistent, patient presence over time literally changes brain physiology in traumatized children
**Family Reunion is Complex** - Both child and family members are traumatized; reunion must be carefully managed to avoid secondary trauma
**Local Context Matters** - Healing must include cultural, community, and family dimensions; external therapy alone is insufficient
**Hope Must Be Maintained** - Karim's connection to his mother—even through brief messages—sustained him through darkest periods of therapy

cases.title: Health Crisis - Diabetes Management in Migration
Family Reunification - Navigating Legal & Emotional Barriers
Readmission & Safe Third Country - Legal Borderlands
Mixed Vulnerabilities 1 - Trauma, Family Conflict & Health Crisis

tuteur_obligations.description: Conduct comprehensive initial assessment across all life domains (health, education, family, psychological, economic)
Ensure TB treatment initiation and monitor medication adherence
Identify and refer to qualified child psychologist or trauma counselor
Facilitate communication between immediate family and extended family
Verify school enrollment and regular attendance
Assess household economic situation and identify available assistance programs
Conduct regular home visits to assess living conditions and safety
Maintain active contact with all service providers (health, school, counselor, etc.)
Support mother's participation in parenting education and stress management
Maintain detailed case file with all documents, assessments, and communications
Ensure child's voices and preferences are heard in decision-making
By month 6, work with family and service providers to plan for transition

lessons_learned: **Multiple Vulnerabilities Interact and Compound**
**Timing of Crisis Intervention is Critical**
**Schools are Critical Early Warning Systems**
**Caregiver Stress is a Protective Factor Risk**
**Family Conflict Resolution can Restore Critical Support**
**Coordinated, Multi-Sectoral Approach is Non-Negotiable**
**Economic Support Must be Paired with Other Interventions**
**Formal Health Systems and Social Protection Systems Must Be Integrated**
**Tailored Support for Different Child Developmental Stages**
**Sustainability Requires Building Family and Community Capacity**

cases.title: Mixed Vulnerabilities 2 - Trafficking, Psychological Trauma & Educational Delay

tuteur_obligations.responsibility: Comprehensive Initial Assessment and Care Planning
tuteur_obligations.description: Assess trafficking experience and trauma impacts across all life domains

tuteur_obligations.responsibility: Medical Care Coordination and Health Monitoring
tuteur_obligations.description: Ensure emergency medical assessment and treatment

tuteur_obligations.responsibility: Psychological Trauma Support and Mental Health Coordination
tuteur_obligations.description: Refer to qualified trauma-informed child psychologist

tuteur_obligations.responsibility: Educational Assessment, Enrollment, and Academic Support
tuteur_obligations.description: Conduct educational assessment; identify learning gaps and needs

tuteur_obligations.responsibility: Family Relationship Building and Reunification Support
tuteur_obligations.description: Assess family relationships and reintegration feasibility

tuteur_obligations.responsibility: Maternal Mental Health and Caregiver Capacity Support
tuteur_obligations.description: Assess mother's mental health status and treatment needs
Assess household economic situation and vulnerabilities
Conduct re-trafficking risk assessment; update periodically
Establish contact with all service providers (medical, psychological, educational, social)
Conduct regular home visits; assess living conditions and safety
Verify Oumou's birth registration and legal documentation
Support Oumou's voice and preferences in decision-making
Track indicators across health, psychological, educational, family, economic domains

lessons_learned: **Trafficking is a Symptom of Poverty and System Failure, Not Primarily a Criminal Issue**
**Families in Extreme Poverty May Participate in Trafficking Unknowingly or Desperately**
**Trafficking Survivors Require Intensive, Specialized, Trauma-Informed Care**
**Educational Delay from Missed Schooling is a Lifelong Vulnerability**
**Maternal Mental Illness Must Be Treated Alongside Family Reunification**
**Poverty Reduction is Re-trafficking Prevention**
**Community Knowledge of Trafficking Event Can Create Stigma or Risk**
**Re-trafficking Risk Assessment Must Be Ongoing, Not One-Time**
**Legal Prosecution of Traffickers Can Conflict with Survivor Protection**
**Trafficking Survivors Require Longer-Term Case Management Than Other Vulnerable Children**

cases.title: Additional Complex Case - Legal, Employment & Family Intersections

tuteur_obligations.responsibility: Comprehensive Initial Assessment and Case Planning
tuteur_obligations.description: Assess abuse, labor exploitation, medical crisis, educational status, family situation
Ensure emergency medical care; oversee infection treatment
Refer to qualified trauma-informed psychologist

tuteur_obligations.responsibility: Educational Assessment, Re-engagement, and Academic Support
tuteur_obligations.description: Conduct educational assessment; identify gaps and learning needs
Assess home environment for safety; identify abuse risks
Assess family relationships and dynamics; document strengths

tuteur_obligations.responsibility: Maternal Support and Domestic Violence Response
tuteur_obligations.description: Assess mother's own trauma and wellbeing
Assess household's economic situation comprehensively
Coordinate with police investigation; ensure proper evidence gathering

tuteur_obligations.responsibility: Labor Abuse Investigation and Employer Accountability
tuteur_obligations.description: Ensure child labor violation is properly investigated
Establish contact with all service providers (medical, school, psychological, legal)
Ensure Bashir's voice and preferences are centered in decision-making
Track indicators across health, psychological, educational, family, economic, legal domains

lessons_learned: **Adolescent Vulnerability is Qualitatively Different from Younger Children**
**Poverty-Driven Child Labor is Systematic, Not Just Individual**
**Occupational Injuries in Child Labor Have Permanent Consequences**
**Domestic Violence Against Mothers Affects Child Protection**
**Educational Delay is Cumulative and Difficult to Reverse**
**Legal Processes Are Slow and Can Re-Traumatize Victims**
**Adolescent Identity Formation is Disrupted by Trauma and Labor**
**Economic Support Must Be Coupled with Employment Support for Caregivers**
**Prosecutorial Response to Child Labor Requires Multi-Sectoral Coordination**
**Recovery from Simultaneous Traumas Takes Time and Patience**
